text: The surgeon begins the procedure on the right side by dissecting the tumor from the undersurface of the liver up to the diaphragm. Once the liver is adequately separated, attention is directed to the medial aspect of the tumor, where a plane is created between the inferior vena cava and the tumor.

The robotic instruments, utilizing endo-wrist movement, are used to precisely dissect the adrenal vein. The vein is clipped with hemolog clips and divided, while bedside assistance ensures safety near major vascular structures.
The surgeon dissects the undersurface of the tumor and the upper pole of the kidney, carefully navigating around major vascular attachments.

The surgeon clips and divides vessels to prevent reactionary bleeding, taking care to preserve the vascular supply to the right kidney. During dissection, caution is exercised to avoid breaching the tumor capsule, with liberal use of clips and bipolar energy.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the bilateral adrenal tumor, aiming to preserve as much of the normal adrenal tissue as possible.

The surgeon identifies a second main adrenal vein draining into the inferior vena cava postrolaterally. The vascular nature of the pheochromocytoma is managed by securing its vascular attachments with hemlock clips and bipolar diathermy.
The surgeon has preserved the normal adrenal tissue to the maximum extent possible. The specimen is packed, and adequate hemostasis is visually confirmed.
Surgicel is placed to control low pressure, low volume bleeding. The specimen is positioned under the left lobe of the liver for easy retrieval during the operation on the opposite side.
The robotic surgical system is repositioned to facilitate surgery on the left side, ensuring optimal access and visualization for the procedure.

The surgeon dissects the left adrenal gland by separating surrounding structures, including the spleen, tail of the pancreas, upper pole of the kidney, and colon, without directly handling the adrenal itself.
The surgeon loops and takes control of the adrenal vein, as part of the adrenal sparing surgery, ensuring the vein is preserved.
The surgeon dissects the tumor-bearing lateral limb of the left adrenal gland and performs enucleoresection to remove the tumor from the adrenal gland.
The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, occasionally using hemolog clips to control bleeding, which is expected during the procedure.
The preserved left adrenal gland is visible, with hemostasis achieved. A surgical drain is placed, and the specimen, including the right-sided adrenal gland, is packed and retrieved.
